---
title: "Continuous Grip-Force Dynamics as an Online Behavioral Signal of Perceptual Strain"
shorttitle: "Grip-Force Dynamics and Perceptual Strain"
leadauthor: "Dastgheib"
author:
  - name: Mohammad Dastgheib
    corresponding: true
    affiliations:
      - Department of Psychology, University of California, Riverside, Riverside, CA, USA
    email: mdastgheib@ucr.edu
abstract: |
  Older adults frequently perform perceptual decisions while sustaining physical effort, yet most dual-task research treats motor output as a binary manipulation rather than a continuous behavioral signal. Here we ask whether sub-second grip-force dynamics carry incremental information about trial-level perceptual accuracy in older adults performing auditory and visual same/different discrimination while maintaining low (5%) or high (40%) isometric handgrip force scaled to maximum voluntary contraction. We extracted event-locked spectral power from the continuous dynamometer stream in a visuomotor tracking band (0.5–3.0 Hz) and a physiological tremor band (8.0–12.0 Hz), then tested whether these features explain perceptual accuracy beyond nominal task difficulty using mixed-effects logistic regression and subject-wise cross-validation. Adding band-power features did not significantly improve model fit (LRT *p* = .447); tremor-band power showed a small numerically negative association with accuracy under both grip conditions (OR ≈ 0.95–0.96), with no significant grip interaction or difficulty interaction. Cross-validated models confirmed only minimal incremental predictive gain from force features beyond task labels (ΔAUC ≈ 0.005), with caveats about model comparability. Exploratory LC microstructure analyses showed non-significant directional trends only. These results establish a reproducible trial-level force-signal pipeline and empirical benchmarks, but do not provide confirmatory evidence for a spectral marker of perceptual strain.
keywords:
  - aging
  - dual-task
  - handgrip force
  - perceptual decision-making
  - physiological tremor
  - cognitive-motor interference
correspondence-email: mdastgheib@ucr.edu
date: "2026-06-24"
bibliography: references.bib
format:
  biorxiv-typst:
    columns: 2
    toc: false
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
---

{{< include _manuscript-content.qmd >}}
